text: The challenging first stage crossing the Pyrenees via the Route Napoléon. Begins with a steep climb through beech forests, reaching the Spanish border at the Col de Lepoeder. Descends steeply to Roncesvalles, an important monastery town.

A long descent from the Pyrenees through the Navarrese countryside. Passes through Zubiri with its medieval bridge over the Arga River, then Villava, birthplace of cycling champion Miguel Induráin. Enters Pamplona through the ancient city walls, famous for the Running of the Bulls during the San Fermín festival.

Leaves Pamplona through the city gates, climbing to the Alto del Perdón with its famous sculpture of pilgrims and wind turbines. Descends through cereal fields and vineyards to the medieval town of Puente la Reina, named for its magnificent 11th-century six-arched bridge over the Arga River.

Follows the Arga and Salado rivers through gentle Navarrese countryside. Passes through Cirauqui, a fortified hilltop village with Roman paving still intact. Crosses vineyards and wheat fields to Estella, the 'Toledo of Navarre,' a beautiful medieval town with Romanesque churches and palaces.

A long stage passing through the famous Irache wine fountain where pilgrims drink free wine. Enters La Rioja wine country through rolling vineyards. Crosses the Ebro River into Logroño, capital of La Rioja, renowned for its tapas street Calle del Laurel and excellent Rioja wines.

Crosses the heart of La Rioja wine region through vineyards and ochre-coloured villages. Passes Navarrete with its 16th-century church containing a painted retablo. Reaches Nájera on the Najerilla River, a town carved into red sandstone cliffs containing the royal pantheon of the Kings of Navarre.

A multi-day stretch through Santo Domingo de la Calzada, named for the medieval engineer-saint who built bridges and roads for pilgrims. Continues through Belorado and San Juan de Ortega before reaching Burgos, dominated by its magnificent Gothic cathedral, a UNESCO World Heritage Site with the tomb of El Cid.

Crosses the Meseta plateau through vast cereal fields and open horizons. The flat, exposed terrain can be challenging in summer heat. Passes through the industrial outskirts of Burgos before reaching the quiet, linear villages typical of the Meseta, ending in Hornillos with its pilgrim hospital tradition.

The long Meseta crossing through Castile. Passes Castrojeriz on a dramatic hill, crosses the Pisuerga River into Palencia, continues through Frómista with its perfect Romanesque church, Carrión de los Condes, and Sahagún before reaching León, a city of stunning stained glass in its Gothic cathedral known as the House of Light.

Initially crosses the urban sprawl of León. Enters the countryside through the Vilar de Mazarife variant or the traditional route via Villadangos. The final approach to Astorga features views of the Maragatería mountains and the city's Episcopal Palace designed by Gaudí.

The demanding ascent into the León mountains via Foncebadón. Passes the Cruz de Ferro at 1504m where pilgrims place stones from home. Descends steeply through El Acebo and Molinaseca to Ponferrada, dominated by its Templar castle built to protect pilgrims.

A pleasant descent through the Bierzo valley, a fertile wine-growing region sheltered by mountains. Passes through Cacabelos and vineyards producing Mencía wine. Villafranca, the 'Little Santiago,' features the Church of Santiago with its Door of Forgiveness for pilgrims unable to continue.

The steep climb into Galicia. Passes through mountain villages like La Faba and Laguna de Castilla before the final demanding ascent to O Cebreiro at 1300m, a village of stone pallozas marking entry into Galicia.

Descends from the mountains through the Galician highlands. Passes Triacastela where pilgrims historically carried limestone to Castañeda. Continues through San Xil or Samos with its enormous 6th-century monastery before reaching Sarria, the last town 100km from Santiago, the minimum distance for a Compostela certificate.

A gentle stage through woodland and farmland, crossing the Minho River via the ancient Roman bridge at Portomarín. Good pilgrim infrastructure with albergues in towns like Barbadelo and Ferreiros. Popular starting point for those walking the minimum 100km to Santiago.

Climbs steadily through oak forests and rural Galicia. The ascent to Monte San Antonio offers panoramic views. Descends to Gonzar before climbing again through woodland to Palas de Rei, historically a royal resting place.

A long stage crossing the province of A Coruña. Passes through the hamlet of Furelos, then Melide where the Camino del Norte joins. The route enters dairy country with green pastures en route to Arzúa, known for its cheese.

A manageable stage through eucalyptus groves and rolling farmland. The Camino Primitivo joins at Arzúa. Gentle ascents and descents through small villages like Santa Irene, with its chapel and healing fountain.

The final stage to the cathedral. Passes through Lavacolla where medieval pilgrims washed. Climbs Monte do Gozo for the first sight of Santiago's spires. Descends through the suburbs to reach Praza do Obradoiro and the Cathedral of Saint James.

The first stage along the rugged Basque coast. Climbs steeply from Irún to the Jaizkibel mountain pass with spectacular views of the Bay of Biscay. Descends through Hondarribia to elegant San Sebastián, famous for its La Concha beach and world-renowned pintxos.

Follows the Basque coastline. Passes through Orio, a traditional fishing village, and Getaria, birthplace of explorer Juan Sebastián Elcano and fashion designer Cristóbal Balenciaga. Ends in Zarautz, a popular beach resort with one of the longest sandy beaches in the Basque Country.

Continues along the dramatic Basque coast. Passes through Zumaia with its famous Flysch rock formations, and the fishing port of Mutriku. The final approach to Deba features views of the bay and the Gothic Church of Santa María.
Turns inland through the Basque mountains. A demanding stage with steep climbs through dense beech and oak forests. Reaches Markina-Xemein, a historic town famous as the birthplace of Basque pelota.

Crosses the rolling hills of Biscay through peaceful countryside. Descends to Gernika, the spiritual heart of the Basque Country, home to the sacred oak tree and site of the 1937 bombing memorialized by Picasso.

A long stage through industrial and natural landscapes. Passes through Portugalete with its Vizcaya Transporter Bridge, a UNESCO World Heritage site. Enters Bilbao, home to the iconic Guggenheim Museum and a revitalized riverfront.

Exits Bilbao through the industrial Basque coastline, gradually transitioning into Cantabria. Passes through Zierbena with its port views and Pobeña beach. Climbs through eucalyptus forests to Castro Urdiales, a picturesque medieval fishing town dominated by the Gothic church of Santa María perched dramatically above the sea.

Enters Cantabria with views of the Cantabrian Sea. Passes through Islares and the spectacular beach at La Arena. Climbs through wooded hills before descending to Laredo, a resort town with one of the longest beaches on the northern coast and a well-preserved medieval quarter.

Crosses the Cantabrian coast, taking a ferry across the Treto estuary to Santoña. Continues through Noja and Galizano with their sandy beaches and fishing villages. Arrives in Santander, the elegant capital of Cantabria, with its belle époque architecture, beaches, and the Magdalena Palace.

Follows the Cantabrian coast through meadows and small fishing villages. Passes through Mogro and Suances on the Saja River estuary. Reaches Santillana del Mar, considered one of Spain's most beautiful towns, a perfectly preserved medieval village near the UNESCO-listed Altamira cave with its 15,000-year-old paintings.

Begins in UNESCO-listed Porto, crossing the iconic Dom Luís I Bridge over the Douro River. Follows urban paths through the city's outskirts, passing through Maia. The route is mostly flat with good surfaces and modern albergue facilities.

Continues through the Portuguese countryside north of Porto. Passes through small farming villages and eucalyptus groves. Reaches Rates with its impressive Romanesque church dedicated to Saint Peter, one of the most important monuments on the Portuguese route.

A gentle stage through the Minho region. Passes through São Pedro de Rates before reaching Barcelos, a historic market town on the Cavado River, famous for the legend of the Rooster of Barcelos and its weekly Thursday market.

A longer stage with gradual climbs through vineyards and cornfields. Descends to Ponte de Lima, one of Portugal's oldest towns, set on the beautiful Lima River with its medieval bridge and Romanesque towers.

A demanding stage with the challenging Labruja mountain crossing. Steep climb through forested hills. Descends to the fortress town of Valença on the Minho River with its impressive 17th-century star-shaped fortifications.

A short stage crossing the International Bridge over the Minho River into Spain. Arrives in Tui, a historic Galician town dominated by its 12th-century fortress cathedral. Marks the transition from Portugal to Galicia.

Enters Galicia through rolling countryside. Passes through O Porriño with its granite stonework tradition. Climbs through woodland to the Alto da Gafa with views of the Ría de Vigo. Descends to Redondela, where the coastal and inland routes of the Portuguese Way converge.

A shorter stage through pleasant rural Galicia. Passes through Arcade, famous for its oysters. Crosses the medieval Ponte Sampaio bridge where Napoleon's army was defeated in 1809. Reaches Pontevedra, an elegant city with one of Spain's finest historic centers.

Crosses the countryside of Pontevedra province. Passes through eucalyptus forests and traditional villages. Reaches Caldas de Reis, a spa town known since Roman times for its thermal waters where pilgrims can soak weary feet.
A gentle stage through Galicia's agricultural region. Arrives in Padrón, legendary landing place of Saint James's boat and famous for its green peppers. Marks the final approach to Santiago.

The final stage into Santiago. Climbs through oak forests to the Chapel of Santiaguiño do Monte with panoramic views. Descends through the suburbs of Santiago, entering the historic center through the Porta do Camiño to the Cathedral of Saint James.
